name: UNO
description: Match colors and numbers, dodge Draw Fours, and be first to empty your hand.
howToPlay: Match the top card of the discard pile by color, number, or symbol.
Use action cards (Skip, Reverse, Draw Two, Wild) to disrupt opponents.
Call "UNO" when you're down to your last card.
First player to empty their hand wins the round.
longDescription:
In this classic, fast-paced card game, players race against one another to empty their hands while forcing opponents to get caught holding high-scoring cards. On each turn, players must carefully evaluate their hand and match the top card of the central discard pile by color, number, or active action symbol. If a player lacks a matching card or valid action, they are forced to draw from the central deck—passing their turn if they remain unable to play.

The game relies heavily on dynamic hand management and disruptive strategic plays driven by special action cards:

- Color and Number Matching: Keep the momentum going by matching the suit or numerical value of the current card on top of the discard pile.

- Action Cards: Discard tactical action cards to force the next player to skip their turn, reverse the order of play, or draw extra cards from the deck.

- Wild Cards: Change the active color at any moment with standard Wild cards, or drop a Wild Draw Four to force an opponent to pick up four cards while redirecting the suit to your advantage.

- Penalties and Calls: Catch opponents off guard when they forget to announce their final remaining card, forcing them to take extra penalty draws from the deck.

Once a player successfully discards their final card, the round ends immediately. Points are scored based on the remaining cards left in opponents' hands, and play continues over multiple rounds until one player achieves the ultimate target score.

name: Anomia
description: Fast-paced matching game where quick reflexes and quicker answers win the round.
howToPlay: Flip cards from your deck one at a time.
When two players' symbols match, race to name an example of the opposing category.
The slower player hands over their card as a penalty.
First to collect a set number of opponents' cards wins.
longDescription:
Anomia plays off the fact that our minds are positively brimming with all sorts of random information—things to eat, pop songs, websites, and more. While it is usually effortless to name a frozen food or a dog breed, your brain works entirely differently under the pressure of a fast-paced head-to-head match!

Players take turns drawing and revealing cards from a central deck into their personal display pile, constantly watching for matching symbols on opponent cards. The core game mechanics include:

- Face-Off Duels: When the symbol on your drawn card matches another player's top card, a face-off instantly begins. You must shout out an example of the category listed on your opponent's card before they can name the category on yours.

- Claiming Rewards: The first player to successfully blurt out a correct answer claims their opponent's top card as a point, placing it face-down in their personal score pile.

- Cascading Face-Offs: Winning a card often reveals a new top card in a player's pile, which can instantly trigger consecutive face-offs across the table in rapid succession.

- Wild Cards: Special wild cards introduce dual-symbol matches between previously non-matching cards, dramatically raising the level of attention and chaos required to stay ahead.

The game continues until the draw piles are completely exhausted, and the player with the most claimed cards at the end of the game wins.

name: Trio
description: Guess the answer before your opponents in this rapid-fire card guessing game.
howToPlay: A category card is revealed to all players.
Each player secretly guesses a number linked to the category.
Reveal simultaneously — closest correct guesses score points.
Most points after all rounds wins.
longDescription:
nana—later re-released as Trio—is a quick-witted deduction and set-collection card game where players compete to locate hidden triplets of identical numbers. The deck consists of 36 cards, featuring numbers 1 through 12 repeated three times each. Players hold a sorted hand of cards arranged strictly from lowest to highest, while the remaining cards lie face-down in a shared central pool on the table.

On a player's turn, they attempt to reveal matching cards one by one by calling out either the lowest or highest card from any player's hand (including their own) or flipping a face-down card from the center. If the first two revealed cards match, the player continues their turn by attempting to find the final matching card; if the numbers differ at any point, all revealed cards return to their original locations and the turn ends immediately.

Successfully revealing all three matching cards allows the active player to claim the set and place it in front of them. Victory can be achieved in several ways depending on the game mode:

- Standard Victory: Secure three complete sets before any opponent.

- Instant Win: Instantly win the game by uncovering the set of 7s, or by collecting two sets whose numbers add up to or subtract to 7 (such as 4s and 11s).

- Spicy Mode (Trio): Shift the focus to high-stakes set combinations, where players win by collecting the 7s or securing two 'linked' sets specified on victory cards.

- Team Play (Trio): Compete in pairs in four- or six-player matches, coordinating card calls and sharing victory requirements with a partner.

name: Exploding Kittens
description: A card game of chance and strategy — avoid the exploding kitten, or defuse it in time.
howToPlay: Draw a card each turn — avoid drawing an Exploding Kitten.
Use action cards to skip, peek, or shuffle the deck to survive.
Defuse an Exploding Kitten if you draw one and have a Defuse card.
Last player left in the game wins.
longDescription:
Exploding Kittens is a highly strategic, kitty-powered version of Russian Roulette. Players take turns drafting cards from a shared deck, moving through the game with caution, as drawing a single Exploding Kitten immediately knocks a player out of the match. The tension scales dramatically with every turn, as the dwindling draw pile steadily increases the mathematical probability of encountering a fatal feline outburst.

The core deck is packed with tactical tools that allow players to manipulate the order of draw cards, mitigate risk, and sabotage opponents before taking a draw step:

- Defuse the Danger: Neutralize drawn Exploding Kittens using laser pointers, catnip sandwiches, or belly rubs to safely insert the kitten back into the deck at any secret location of your choice.

- Attack and Force Turns: Pass your mandatory draw step to the next player while forcing them to take multiple consecutive turns in a row.

- Manipulate the Deck: Use specialized action cards to peek at the top three cards of the deck, alter their order, or thoroughly shuffle the draw pile to disrupt an opponent's turn.

- Skip and Steal: Bypass your draw requirement instantly with Skip cards, or play matching Cat card pairs to steal valuable defuses and utility cards directly from an opponent's hand.

- Cancel Opponent Actions: Play Nope cards at any moment to invalidate an opponent's action, creating chaotic chains of counters and re-counters.

Players continue managing their hand, neutralizing threats, and directing danger toward rivals until only one survivor remains standing, declaring them the ultimate winner.

name: Sequence
description: Blend cards and board play to form a sequence of five chips before your rivals.
howToPlay: Play a card from your hand and place a chip on the matching board space.
Jacks let you place or remove chips freely.
Form a line of five chips — horizontally, vertically, or diagonally.
First team or player to complete the required sequences wins.
longDescription:
Sequence is an engaging strategy game that seamlessly blends card play with classic board-positioning mechanics. The game board displays every card face—excluding the Jacks—from two standard 52-card decks, organized into a clean 10x10 grid. The four corner spaces act as neutral free zones, counting as an active chip for every player simultaneously to help build tactical advantage.

Players or teams take turns playing a card from their hand to place a colored marker chip onto a matching space on the board, working to connect chips while blocking their opponents' momentum. The game revolves around several key tactical elements:

- Forming Sequences: Race to align five continuous marker chips of the same color horizontally, vertically, or diagonally across the board.

- Wild Two-Eyed Jacks: Play a Two-Eyed Jack as a wild card to place a marker chip on any open space on the board, filling critical gaps in your line.

- Removal One-Eyed Jacks: Play a One-Eyed Jack to target and remove an opponent's marker chip from the board, frustrating their plans and breaking up potential sequences.

- Neutral Corners: Utilize the four open corner spaces strategically, as they serve as free chips for all players to complete overlapping lines.

The game ends instantly when a player or team successfully completes the required number of five-chip sequences, claiming victory over the board.

name: Splendor
description: Collect gems, build your merchant empire, and outpace rival traders.
howToPlay: Collect gem tokens or reserve development cards each turn.
Spend gems to purchase cards that grant permanent bonuses.
Attract nobles by meeting their card requirements for bonus points.
First to 15 prestige points triggers the final round — highest score wins.
longDescription:
Splendor is a game of chip-collecting and card development. Players are merchants of the Renaissance trying to buy gem mines, means of transportation, shops—all in order to acquire the most prestige points. If you're wealthy enough, you might even receive a visit from a noble at some point, which of course will further increase your prestige.

On your turn, you may (1) collect chips (gems), or (2) buy and build a card, or (3) reserve one card. If you collect chips, you take either three different kinds of chips or two chips of the same kind. If you buy a card, you pay its price in chips and add it to your playing area. To reserve a card—in order to make sure you get it, or, why not, your opponents don't get it—you place it in front of you face down for later building; this costs you a round, but you also get gold in the form of a joker chip, which you can use as any gem.

All of the cards you buy increase your wealth as they give you a permanent gem bonus for later buys; some of the cards also give you prestige points. In order to win the game, you must reach 15 prestige points before your opponents do.

name: Azul
description: Draft glowing tiles to craft the most elegant palace wall in the realm.
howToPlay: Draft tiles of a single color from factory displays each round.
Place drafted tiles onto your staging rows.
Completed rows move tiles onto your wall for scoring, penalizing overflow.
Game ends when a player completes a horizontal wall row — highest score wins.
longDescription:
Introduced by the Moors, azulejos (originally white and blue ceramic tiles) were fully embraced by the Portuguese when their king Manuel I, on a visit to the Alhambra palace in Southern Spain, was mesmerized by the stunning beauty of the Moorish decorative tiles. The king, awestruck by the interior beauty of the Alhambra, immediately ordered that his own palace in Portugal be decorated with similar wall tiles. As a tile-laying artist, you have been challenged to embellish the walls of the Royal Palace of Evora.

In the game Azul, players take turns drafting colored tiles from suppliers to their player board. Later in the round, players score points based on how they've placed their tiles to decorate the palace. Extra points are scored for specific patterns and completing sets; wasted supplies harm the player's score. The player with the most points at the end of the game wins.

name: Catan
description: Trade, build and settle your way to the largest empire on the island.
howToPlay: Roll dice each turn to generate resources from adjacent terrain.
Trade resources with other players or the bank.
Spend resources to build roads, settlements, and cities.
First player to reach 10 victory points wins.
longDescription:
In CATAN, players strive to become the dominant force on the island of Catan by building settlements, cities, and connecting roads across an ever-changing landscape. The island is created by randomly arranging large hexagonal terrain tiles representing rich natural landscapes—forests, hills, pastures, fields, and mountains—surrounded by a vast ocean. Each turn, the roll of two six-sided dice determines which territories yield their valuable resources, driving a vibrant player economy.

Players spend their harvested resource cards—wood, brick, sheep, wheat, and ore—to construct infrastructure, expand their reach, and earn victory points. The first player to reach 10 victory points on their turn claims ultimate victory over the island.

On their turn, a player executes a series of strategic phases:

- Harvest Resources: Roll two dice to generate resources for every player with settlements or cities adjacent to terrain tiles matching the rolled number.

- Trade with Players or Ports: Negotiate domestic resource trades with opponents, or use coastal ports and bank trades to exchange excess goods for missing materials.

- Build and Expand: Spend resource combinations to build new roads, found settlements, upgrade existing settlements into high-yielding cities, or purchase versatile Development Cards.

- Unleash the Robber: Rolling a 7 halts resource production, forces players with oversized hands to discard half their cards, and allows the active player to move the robber pawn to block an opponent's tile and steal a resource.

- Accumulate Victory Points: Earn points from physical settlements and cities, secret Development Cards, or by securing dynamic special achievements like the Longest Road or Largest Army.

name: Cascadia
description: Lay habitat tiles and place wildlife to build the most harmonious nature reserve.
howToPlay: Draft a paired habitat tile and wildlife token each turn.
Place the tile to expand your landscape and the token onto matching habitat.
Score points for wildlife groupings and habitat corridors at game end.
Highest combined score across all scoring categories wins.
longDescription:
Set in the breathtaking Pacific Northwest, Cascadia is a puzzly tile-laying and token-drafting game centered on creating harmonious ecosystems. Players compete to build the most diverse and interconnected terrain area, balancing the growth of sprawling natural habitats with the introduction of native wildlife species.

Each player begins their journey with a starting triplet of hexagonal habitat tiles featuring various terrains, such as mountains, forests, prairies, wetlands, and rivers. On your turn, you must draft a dynamic duo from a central market: a single habitat tile paired randomly with a wildlife token. The game rewards spatial optimization, as you strive to expand matching biomes while providing suitable homes for the region's animal residents.

Players must balance two distinct scoring mechanics as they expand their environments:

- Build Habitat Corridors: Connect matching terrain types to reduce fragmentation and build massive contiguous biomes, scoring points for your largest area of each habitat along with potential majority bonuses.

- Populate Wildlife: Place drafted animal tokens—representing bears, elks, salmon, hawks, and foxes—onto valid tiles to satisfy distinct scoring cards that change every game.

- Spend Nature Tokens: Utilize rare pinecone tokens earned by placing animals on specialized tiles to refresh the market or freely mix and match any tile and token on display.

- Optimize Animal Formations: Position your wildlife to meet their specific social desires, whether that means clustering salmon into long rivers, pairing up grizzly bears, or keeping majestic hawks isolated. The player who creates the most cohesive ecosystem and scores the most combined points wins.

name: Wingspan
description: Attract birds to your wildlife preserves in this beautifully engine-building game.
howToPlay: Play bird cards into forest, grassland, or wetland habitats.
Activate habitat rows to gain food, lay eggs, or draw cards.
Chain bird abilities together to build a powerful engine.
Highest score after 4 rounds — from birds, eggs, food, and bonuses — wins.
longDescription:
Wingspan is a competitive, medium-weight, card-driven engine-building board game where players step into the shoes of passionate bird enthusiasts. As researchers, bird watchers, ornithologists, and collectors, your goal is to discover and attract the most spectacular birds to your expanding network of wildlife preserves. Every bird you attract extends a chain of powerful combinations within your distinct habitats, driving your ecological engine forward.

The game plays out over four rounds, with players managing their preserves across three specialized habitats. Each habitat corresponds to a fundamental action necessary for growth, allowing players to build increasingly complex strategies as their sanctuaries fill with life.

During the game, players manage their preserves through three key actions:

- Gain food tokens via custom dice rolled inside a thematic birdfeeder dice tower.

- Lay eggs using colorful egg miniatures to populate nests and fund future bird placements.

- Draw from hundreds of unique, beautifully illustrated bird cards and play them into their matching habitats.

- Complete diverse end-of-round goals and secret bonus objectives to maximize their efficiency. The winner is the player who has accumulated the most total points from birds, eggs, cached food, tucked cards, and objectives after the fourth round concludes.

name: 7 Wonders
description: Draft cards across three ages to build a civilization that stands the test of time.
howToPlay: Draft one card from your hand each turn, then pass the rest along.
Build structures, military, or wonder stages using resources.
Compare military strength with neighbors at the end of each age.
Highest total score from civilian, military, science, and wonders wins.
longDescription:
As the leader of one of the seven greatest cities of the Ancient World, you are tasked with gathering essential resources, developing prosperous commercial routes, and securing your military supremacy. Over the course of three generations, you will build up your city and erect a magnificent architectural wonder that will transcend future times.

7 Wonders is a fast-paced card development and drafting game where players build their civilizations simultaneously. Each player commands a unique, double-sided player board that grants distinct special powers and tracks their wonder's construction progress. The game concludes at the end of the third age, and the player who has accumulated the most victory points through science, military, commerce, and civic buildings wins.

The gameplay flows through a continuous cycle of drafting and passing cards:

- Draft and Reveal: At the start of an age, players receive seven cards, secretly choose one to play, and simultaneously reveal their choices. Cards can be built for free, paid for with resources, or discarded for coins.

- Pass and Repeat: After revealing their card, players pass their remaining hand to their neighbor. This process repeats until everyone has placed six cards into their city, at which point the current age ends and military conflicts are resolved.

- Shifting Directions: Cards are passed left in the first age, right in the second, and left again in the third. Because every drafted card is played immediately, you must constantly monitor the developing strategies of the neighbors on both sides of you.

- Flexible Scalability: While the game beautifully accommodates large groups of up to seven players with zero downtime, the rules also include an official, dedicated two-player variant.

name: Cryptid
description: Use logic and sly deduction to be the first to pinpoint the creature's hidden location.
howToPlay: Each player holds a private clue about the creature's location.
Ask other players questions to gather information, revealed via their clue.
Eliminate map spaces using logic until one remains.
First to correctly guess the creature's exact location wins.
longDescription:
You have studied the footage, connected the dots, and gathered what meager evidence you could to finally reveal the truth behind the Cryptid. A group of like-minded cryptozoologists have united to uncover the elusive creature, but the glory of discovery is far too rich to share. To locate the beast, you must carefully share fragments of your knowledge without giving away so much that your opponents beat you to the find.

Cryptid is a unique deduction game of honest misdirection where players attempt to uncover their opponents' secret clues while throwing them off the scent of their own. Each player holds exactly one piece of evidence that helps pinpoint the creature's location on the map, and players spend their turns gathering extra information from the rest of the group.

The game moves through an engaging puzzle of questioning and deduction:

- Questioning: On your turn, you can ask a specific player about a precise tile on the map, forcing them to honestly reveal whether the Cryptid could exist there according to their secret clue.

- Searching: Alternatively, you can declare a search on a space you believe is correct, forcing all players to reveal in turn order if the tile matches their evidence until the beast is found or a clue contradicts it.

The game features a modular board, five clue books, and a deck of cards offering hundreds of setups across two difficulty levels. It is also supported by an optional companion app for faster setup and a near-infinite range of puzzles. The first player to successfully deduce the exact tile where the creature hides wins the game.

name: Heat: Pedal to the Metal
description: Race, draft gears, and manage your engine heat to cross the finish line first.
howToPlay: Play speed cards from your hand to move your car along the track.
Shift gears and manage heat cards to handle corners without spinning out.
Slipstream behind rivals for extra speed on straights.
First car to complete all laps wins the race.
longDescription:
Based on simple and intuitive hand management, Heat: Pedal to the Metal puts players in the driver's seat of intense 1960s car races, jockeying for position to cross the finish line first while constantly managing their vehicle's heat engine. Drivers must carefully regulate their speed through tight corners to avoid overheating, shifting gears to maintain momentum without blowing their engine on the straightaways.

Selecting the right upgrades and tuning options helps drivers hug sharp turns, clear chicanes, and keep their engine cool enough to sustain top speeds. The game features multiple dynamic modes and tactical modules to enhance every race:

- Gear and Heat Management: Play speed cards to advance along the track, paying heat cards into your engine pool when shifting rapidly or pushing past safe corner limits.

- Upgrades and Customization: Tailor your race car with special utility cards—such as improved brakes, cooling systems, or superchargers—to optimize performance for specific track layouts.

- Championship System: Experience a full racing season in a single game night, complete with changing weather conditions, shifting road variables, and event cards that alter track rules between races.

- Solo Play and Bot Racers: Tackle the track alone using the robust Legends Module, or seamlessly integrate automated drivers to fill out the grid in multiplayer matches.

Ultimately, a driver's ability to balance aggressive speed with calculated thermal control will be the key to taking the checkered flag and claiming the top spot on the podium.

name: Everdell
description: Build a woodland city of critters and constructions across four charming seasons.
howToPlay: Gather resources by placing workers on the board each season.
Play critter and construction cards to build your city.
Chain card abilities together to grow your engine efficiently.
Highest points from your city, events, and journey at game end wins.
longDescription:
Deep within the charming valley of Everdell, beneath the boughs of towering trees and beside meandering streams, a civilization of forest critters is thriving. After many years of peace, the time has come to settle new territories and establish grand new cities. As the leader of a group of ambitious critters, you are tasked with constructing buildings, meeting lively characters, and hosting grand events before the winter moon rises.

Everdell is a dynamic game of tableau building and worker placement that spans four seasons. Players work to develop their own woodland settlements, balancing their resources to construct the most prosperous city in the valley. The game ends at the onset of the following winter, at which point the player with the highest-scoring city wins.

On their turn, a player can take one of three distinct actions:

- Place a Worker: Players deploy their worker pieces onto various board locations, event tiles, or Destination cards. Workers perform critical development tasks, such as gathering essential resources, drawing new cards, and executing special actions.

- Play a Card: Players build and populate their city by adding up to 15 Construction and Critter cards to their tableau. Cards belong to five unique types—Travelers, Production, Destination, Governance, and Prosperity—which generate resources (twigs, resin, pebbles, and berries), grant powerful abilities, and score victory points.

- Prepare for the next Season: Players recall their workers from the board and gain new workers for the upcoming season, unlocking new strategic opportunities as the year progresses.

name: Castles Of Burgundy
description: Roll, place, and build your way to the most prosperous estate in Burgundy.
howToPlay: Roll dice to select tiles from the central display each turn.
Place tiles onto your player board to complete regions.
Complete regions to trigger bonus actions and score points.
Highest total score after five phases wins.
longDescription:
Set in the Burgundy region of High Medieval France, players act as aristocrats seeking to expand their small princedoms. By drafting settlement tiles from a central board and placing them onto their personal map, players build powerful castles, establish trade, exploit silver mines, and deploy unique tile abilities to maximize their kingdom's potential.

The game spans five phases, each consisting of five rounds. Every round begins with players rolling two personal dice, which dictate the actions they can perform. A first-player goods die determines where new resources appear on the board, adding a layer of tactical planning to every round.

On their turn, players spend their dice to perform two of four core actions:

- Draft a settlement tile from the main board into their temporary staging area.

- Move a tile from the staging area into a matching, adjacent region of their princedom.

- Deliver goods from their supply that match the face value of a die.

- Hire worker tokens, which can be spent later to alter future dice rolls.

Additionally, players can spend silver once per turn to buy a tile from the central depot. The game concludes after the final phase, where victory points are tallied from completed regions, undelivered goods, leftover resources, and end-game bonus tiles. The aristocrat with the most points wins.

name: Brass Birmingham
description: Build industries and networks across two eras of the Industrial Revolution.
howToPlay: Build industry tiles and canal or rail links across the map.
Sell goods to generate income and score victory points.
Develop and upgrade industries to unlock stronger tiles.
Highest combined score across both eras wins.
longDescription:
In the smoke-filled skies of the Industrial Revolution, competing entrepreneurs fight for dominance in Brass: Birmingham, the economic strategy sequel to Martin Wallace's 2007 masterpiece. Set between the years of 1770 and 1870, the game tasks players with developing, building, and establishing complex industrial networks to exploit shifting market demands.

While sharing core mechanics with its predecessor, it delivers a distinctly different strategic arc and experience. The competition unfolds across two distinct historical halves—the canal era (1770-1830) and the rail era (1830-1870). The ultimate goal is to accumulate the most Victory Points (VPs), which are calculated at the end of each era based on your active canal or rail links and your successfully established, flipped industry tiles.

During each round, players take turns dictated by a dynamic turn order track, spending two actions to expand their empire. Players can choose from the five classic actions:

1) Build - Pay required resources and place an industry tile.

2) Network - Add a rail / canal link, expanding your network.

3) Develop - Increase the VP value of an industry.

4) Sell - Sell your cotton, manufactured goods and pottery.

5) Loan - Take a £30 loan and reduce your income.

Additionally, Brass: Birmingham introduces a completely new strategic option that replaces the original game's Double Action Build:

6) Scout - Discard three cards and take a wild location and wild industry card.

name: Blood on the Clocktower
description: A narrator-led social deduction game of secret roles, bluffing, and nightly murder.
howToPlay: Each player is secretly assigned a good or evil role by the Storyteller.
By night, evil roles act in secret; by day, the town debates and votes.
Use claimed information and bluffing to identify the evil players.
Good wins by executing all evil players; evil wins by eliminating enough good players.
longDescription:
In the quiet town of Ravenswood Bluff, a hidden Demon is murdering the residents under the cover of night. By day, this Demon disguises themselves as a regular townsfolk.

This is the premise of Blood on the Clocktower, a social bluffing game where players split into two opposing teams: Good and Evil. A designated Storyteller oversees the game, managing the action and making critical decisions behind the scenes. The Good team wins if they can deduce who the Demon is and execute them before Evil outnumbers the town.

The game moves between day and night phases:

Day Phase: Players talk openly or whisper privately to share clues or spread lies. The day ends with a town vote to execute a suspected Evil player.

Night Phase: Everyone closes their eyes, and the Storyteller wakes players up one by one to use their unique abilities—whether that means gathering info, causing mischief, or committing murders.

Because the Storyteller manages all the physical game pieces, the rest of the group can play without needing a table or board. Every player gets a completely unique role with its own special ability. If your character dies, you stay in the game as a ghost and can still help your team win. Even late arrivals can join the game as "Travellers"—powerful characters with unpredictable loyalties.
